_note: Snapshot of federal officeholders as of the start of the 119th Congress and the second Trump administration. Update this file when cabinet secretaries, Supreme Court justices, or congressional leadership change. Historical predecessors are retained in 'predecessor' fields for context.

president.name: Donald J. Trump
president.selection_detail: Elected November 5, 2024; inaugurated January 20, 2025 as the 47th President of the United States. Previously served as the 45th President (2017–2021).

contact.dc_office: The White House, 1600 Pennsylvania Avenue NW, Washington, DC 20500 (Oval Office)

top_issues.name: Trade & Tariffs
top_issues.stance: Administration priority on protective tariffs and renegotiating trade agreements to favor domestic manufacturing.

top_issues.name: Immigration & Border Security
top_issues.stance: Emphasizes increased border enforcement and tighter limits on asylum and legal immigration.

top_issues.name: Energy
top_issues.stance: Promotes expanded domestic oil, gas, and coal production and reduced environmental regulation.

top_issues.name: Government Reform
top_issues.stance: Supports reducing the size of the federal workforce and restructuring federal agencies.

experience.note: The Apprentice and Celebrity Apprentice host

experience.role: New York City Real Estate Investor

president.bio: Donald John Trump is the 47th president of the United States, inaugurated on January 20, 2025, after winning the 2024 election. He previously served as the 45th president from 2017 to 2021. Before entering politics, he was a real-estate developer and businessman who led the Trump Organization and hosted the reality television series “The Apprentice.”

vice_president.name: JD Vance
vice_president.role: Vice President of the United States
vice_president.selection_detail: Elected as Trump's running mate on Nov 5, 2024. Previously a U.S. Senator from Ohio (2023–2025). Serves as President of the Senate ex officio.

contact.dc_office: Eisenhower Executive Office Building, 17th Street & Pennsylvania Avenue NW, Washington, DC 20500

top_issues.name: Administration Agenda
top_issues.stance: As Vice President, advances the administration's policy agenda and serves as President of the Senate.

top_issues.name: Trade & Manufacturing
top_issues.stance: Advocates policies oriented toward domestic manufacturing and working-class economic concerns.

top_issues.name: Immigration & Border Security
top_issues.stance: Supports stricter border enforcement and immigration limits.

experience.role: Vice President of the United States

vice_president.bio: JD Vance is the 50th vice president of the United States, inaugurated on January 20, 2025. He represented Ohio in the United States Senate from 2023 to 2025. Earlier in his career he served in the U.S. Marine Corps, worked as a venture capitalist, and wrote the best-selling memoir “Hillbilly Elegy.”

cabinet.name: Marco Rubio
cabinet.selection_detail: Nominated by President Trump, confirmed by the Senate 99–0 on Jan 20, 2025. Previously a U.S. Senator from Florida (2011–2025).

contact.dc_office: Harry S Truman Building, 2201 C Street NW, Washington, DC 20520

top_issues.name: Foreign Policy & Diplomacy
top_issues.stance: Leads the Department of State; oversees U.S. diplomacy, embassies, and treaty relations.

top_issues.name: National Security
top_issues.stance: Focus on relations with China and on countering foreign adversaries.

top_issues.name: Western Hemisphere
top_issues.stance: Longstanding focus on Latin America policy, including Cuba and Venezuela.

cabinet.bio: Marco Rubio is the U.S. secretary of state, sworn in in January 2025. He represented Florida in the United States Senate from 2011 until joining the Cabinet, sought the Republican presidential nomination in 2016, and earlier served as speaker of the Florida House of Representatives.

cabinet.name: Scott Bessent
cabinet.selection_detail: Hedge-fund manager and founder of Key Square Group. Confirmed by the Senate 68–29 on Jan 27, 2025.

contact.dc_office: Main Treasury Building, 1500 Pennsylvania Avenue NW, Washington, DC 20220

top_issues.name: Economic & Fiscal Policy
top_issues.stance: Leads the Treasury Department; oversees federal finances, tax administration, and debt management.

top_issues.name: Tax & Trade
top_issues.stance: Role in tax policy and implementation of tariff and trade measures.

top_issues.name: Financial Regulation & Sanctions
top_issues.stance: Oversees financial-system stability and the administration of economic sanctions.

cabinet.bio: Scott Bessent is the U.S. secretary of the Treasury, confirmed in January 2025. A longtime investor, he founded the hedge fund Key Square Group and previously served as chief investment officer at Soros Fund Management.

cabinet.name: Pete Hegseth
cabinet.selection_detail: Former Army National Guard officer and Fox News host. Confirmed by the Senate 51–50 on Jan 24, 2025 with VP Vance casting the tiebreaking vote.

contact.dc_office: The Pentagon, 1400 Defense Pentagon, Washington, DC 20301

top_issues.name: National Defense
top_issues.stance: Leads the Department of Defense and the U.S. armed forces.

top_issues.name: Military Readiness
top_issues.stance: Stated focus on warfighting readiness and recruitment.

top_issues.name: Defense Management
top_issues.stance: Oversees the defense budget and military personnel policy.

cabinet.bio: Pete Hegseth is the U.S. secretary of defense, confirmed in January 2025. An Army National Guard veteran who deployed to Iraq, Afghanistan, and Guantanamo Bay, he was previously a weekend host on Fox News and led veterans’ advocacy organizations.

cabinet.name: Pam Bondi
cabinet.selection_detail: Former Attorney General of Florida (2011–2019). Confirmed by the Senate 54–46 on Feb 4, 2025 after Trump's initial nominee, Matt Gaetz, withdrew.

contact.dc_office: Robert F. Kennedy Department of Justice Building, 950 Pennsylvania Avenue NW, Washington, DC 20530

top_issues.name: Federal Law Enforcement
top_issues.stance: Leads the Department of Justice; oversees federal prosecutions and law enforcement.

top_issues.name: Violent Crime & Drugs
top_issues.stance: Focus on violent crime and drug trafficking, including fentanyl; former Florida Attorney General.

top_issues.name: Immigration Enforcement
top_issues.stance: Oversees immigration-related federal prosecutions.

cabinet.bio: Pam Bondi is the U.S. attorney general, confirmed in February 2025. She served as the attorney general of Florida from 2011 to 2019 and earlier worked for nearly two decades as a prosecutor in the Tampa, Florida, area.

cabinet.name: Doug Burgum
cabinet.selection_detail: Former Governor of North Dakota (2016–2024). Confirmed by the Senate 79–18 on Jan 30, 2025.

contact.dc_office: Main Interior Building, 1849 C Street NW, Washington, DC 20240

top_issues.name: Public Lands & Natural Resources
top_issues.stance: Leads the Interior Department; manages federal lands, national parks, and natural resources.

top_issues.name: Energy on Federal Lands
top_issues.stance: Focus on expanding domestic energy production on public lands.

top_issues.name: Tribal Affairs
top_issues.stance: Oversees the federal government's relationship with Native American tribes.

experience.role: Business Executive, Great Plains Software & Microsoft

cabinet.bio: Doug Burgum is the U.S. secretary of the interior, confirmed in 2025. He served as governor of North Dakota from 2016 to 2024 and earlier built and led the business-software company Great Plains, which was acquired by Microsoft.

cabinet.name: Brooke Rollins
cabinet.selection_detail: Previously headed the America First Policy Institute. Confirmed by the Senate 72–28 on Feb 13, 2025.

contact.dc_office: Jamie L. Whitten Building, 1400 Independence Avenue SW, Washington, DC 20250

top_issues.name: Agriculture & Farm Policy
top_issues.stance: Leads the USDA; oversees farm programs, food safety, and forestry.

top_issues.name: Rural Development
top_issues.stance: Oversees rural development and agricultural trade programs.

top_issues.name: Nutrition Assistance
top_issues.stance: Administers federal nutrition programs, including SNAP and school meals.

cabinet.bio: Brooke Rollins is the U.S. secretary of agriculture, confirmed in 2025. She previously led the America First Policy Institute, served as a domestic-policy adviser in the first Trump administration, and earlier worked in Texas state government.

cabinet.name: Howard Lutnick
cabinet.selection_detail: Chairman and CEO of Cantor Fitzgerald. Confirmed by the Senate 51–45 on Feb 18, 2025.

contact.dc_office: Herbert C. Hoover Building, 1401 Constitution Avenue NW, Washington, DC 20230

top_issues.name: Commerce & Trade
top_issues.stance: Leads the Commerce Department; oversees trade, economic statistics, and the Census.

top_issues.name: Tariffs & Industry
top_issues.stance: Central role in administration tariff and trade policy.

top_issues.name: Technology & Export Controls
top_issues.stance: Oversees export administration and standards through agencies such as NIST and BIS.

cabinet.bio: Howard Lutnick is the U.S. secretary of commerce, confirmed in 2025. He is the longtime chairman and chief executive of the financial-services firm Cantor Fitzgerald, which he rebuilt after the September 11, 2001, attacks.

cabinet.name: Lori Chavez-DeRemer
cabinet.selection_detail: Former U.S. Representative from Oregon (2023–2025). Confirmed by the Senate 67–32 on Mar 10, 2025.

contact.dc_office: Frances Perkins Building, 200 Constitution Avenue NW, Washington, DC 20210

top_issues.name: Labor & Workforce
top_issues.stance: Leads the Department of Labor; oversees workplace standards, wages, and worker protections.

top_issues.name: Job Training
top_issues.stance: Oversees federal workforce-development and apprenticeship programs.

top_issues.name: Workplace Safety
top_issues.stance: Administers occupational safety and health enforcement (OSHA).

cabinet.bio: Lori Chavez-DeRemer is the U.S. secretary of labor, confirmed in 2025. She represented Oregon’s 5th congressional district in the U.S. House from 2023 to 2025 and earlier served as mayor of Happy Valley, Oregon.

cabinet.name: Robert F. Kennedy Jr.
cabinet.role: Secretary of Health and Human Services
cabinet.department: Department of Health and Human Services
cabinet.selection_detail: Former Democrat and 2024 independent presidential candidate. Confirmed by the Senate 52–48 on Feb 13, 2025.

contact.dc_office: Hubert H. Humphrey Building, 200 Independence Avenue SW, Washington, DC 20201

top_issues.name: Public Health
top_issues.stance: Leads HHS; oversees Medicare, Medicaid, the FDA, CDC, and NIH.

top_issues.name: Chronic Disease & Nutrition
top_issues.stance: Stated focus on chronic disease, food additives, and nutrition policy.

top_issues.name: Health Agency Oversight
top_issues.stance: Oversees federal public-health and biomedical-research agencies.

experience.role: Secretary of Health and Human Services

cabinet.bio: Robert F. Kennedy Jr. is the U.S. secretary of health and human services, confirmed in February 2025. An environmental lawyer and activist for several decades, he founded the group Children’s Health Defense and ran for president in 2024 before endorsing Donald Trump.

cabinet.name: Scott Turner
cabinet.role: Secretary of Housing and Urban Development
cabinet.department: Department of Housing and Urban Development
cabinet.selection_detail: Former NFL player and Texas state representative; led Opportunity and Revitalization Council in Trump's first term. Confirmed 55–44 on Feb 5, 2025.

contact.dc_office: Robert C. Weaver Federal Building, 451 7th Street SW, Washington, DC 20410

top_issues.name: Housing Policy
top_issues.stance: Leads HUD; oversees federal housing programs and community development.

top_issues.name: Affordable Housing
top_issues.stance: Focus on housing access and affordability.

top_issues.name: Economic Opportunity
top_issues.stance: Previously led the White House Opportunity and Revitalization Council (Opportunity Zones).

experience.role: Secretary of Housing and Urban Development

cabinet.bio: Scott Turner is the U.S. secretary of housing and urban development, confirmed in 2025. A former Texas state representative, he led the White House Opportunity and Revitalization Council during the first Trump administration and earlier played in the National Football League.

cabinet.name: Sean Duffy
cabinet.selection_detail: Former U.S. Representative from Wisconsin (2011–2019). Confirmed by the Senate 77–22 on Jan 28, 2025.

contact.dc_office: 1200 New Jersey Avenue SE, Washington, DC 20590

top_issues.name: Transportation & Infrastructure
top_issues.stance: Leads the DOT; oversees highways, aviation, rail, and transit.

top_issues.name: Aviation Safety
top_issues.stance: Oversees the Federal Aviation Administration and air-traffic system.

top_issues.name: Infrastructure Programs
top_issues.stance: Administers federal transportation funding and safety programs.

cabinet.bio: Sean Duffy is the U.S. secretary of transportation, confirmed in 2025. He represented Wisconsin’s 7th congressional district in the U.S. House from 2011 to 2019, served as a county prosecutor, and later worked as a television commentator.

cabinet.name: Chris Wright
cabinet.selection_detail: Founder and CEO of Liberty Energy (oilfield services). Confirmed by the Senate 59–38 on Feb 3, 2025.

contact.dc_office: James V. Forrestal Building, 1000 Independence Avenue SW, Washington, DC 20585

top_issues.name: Energy Policy
top_issues.stance: Leads the Department of Energy; oversees energy production, the grid, and the national labs.

top_issues.name: Domestic Energy & Nuclear
top_issues.stance: Focus on expanding domestic energy production, including oil, gas, and nuclear.

top_issues.name: Nuclear Security
top_issues.stance: Oversees the National Nuclear Security Administration.

cabinet.bio: Chris Wright is the U.S. secretary of energy, confirmed in February 2025. He founded and led Liberty Energy, an oilfield-services company, and has worked in the energy industry for decades.

cabinet.name: Linda McMahon
cabinet.selection_detail: Co-founder of WWE; led the Small Business Administration during Trump's first term. Confirmed 51–45 on Mar 3, 2025. Trump signed an executive order in March 2025 directing the department's dismantlement.

contact.dc_office: Lyndon Baines Johnson Department of Education Building, 400 Maryland Avenue SW, Washington, DC 20202

top_issues.name: Education Policy
top_issues.stance: Leads the Department of Education; oversees federal education funding and student aid.

top_issues.name: Federal Role in Education
top_issues.stance: Administration has stated an intent to reduce the federal role in K-12 education.

top_issues.name: Student Aid
top_issues.stance: Oversees federal student-loan and financial-aid programs.

cabinet.bio: Linda McMahon is the U.S. secretary of education, confirmed in March 2025. She co-founded World Wrestling Entertainment (WWE) and led the U.S. Small Business Administration during the first Trump administration.

cabinet.name: Doug Collins
cabinet.selection_detail: Former U.S. Representative from Georgia (2013–2021) and Air Force Reserve chaplain. Confirmed 77–23 on Feb 4, 2025.

contact.dc_office: Veterans Affairs Central Office, 810 Vermont Avenue NW, Washington, DC 20420

top_issues.name: Veterans Affairs
top_issues.stance: Leads the VA; oversees veterans' health care, benefits, and national cemeteries.

top_issues.name: Veterans Health Care
top_issues.stance: Oversees the Veterans Health Administration.

top_issues.name: Veterans Benefits
top_issues.stance: Administers disability, education, and pension benefits for veterans.

experience.role: Major, U.S. Air Force Reserve Chaplain

cabinet.bio: Doug Collins is the U.S. secretary of veterans affairs, confirmed in February 2025. He represented Georgia’s 9th congressional district in the U.S. House from 2013 to 2021 and serves as a chaplain in the U.S. Air Force Reserve.

cabinet.name: Kristi Noem
cabinet.selection_detail: Former Governor of South Dakota (2019–2025). Confirmed by the Senate 59–34 on Jan 25, 2025.

contact.dc_office: St. Elizabeths West Campus, 2707 Martin Luther King Jr. Avenue SE, Washington, DC 20528

top_issues.name: Homeland Security
top_issues.stance: Leads DHS; oversees border security, immigration enforcement, TSA, FEMA, and the Secret Service.

top_issues.name: Border & Immigration
top_issues.stance: Focus on border enforcement and immigration policy.

top_issues.name: Disaster Response
top_issues.stance: Oversees FEMA and federal disaster response and recovery.

cabinet.bio: Kristi Noem is the U.S. secretary of homeland security, confirmed in January 2025. She served as governor of South Dakota from 2019 to 2025 and earlier represented the state in the U.S. House from 2011 to 2019.

supreme_court.body_name: Supreme Court of the United States
supreme_court._note: Justices are nominated by the President, confirmed by the Senate, and hold their office during good behavior (effectively for life).

members.name: John G. Roberts Jr.
members.selection_detail: Nominated by President George W. Bush to the Chief Justice seat after William Rehnquist's death. Confirmed by the Senate 78–22 on Sep 29, 2005.

contact.dc_office: Supreme Court of the United States, 1 First Street NE, Washington, DC 20543

top_issues.name: Federal Judiciary & the Court's Role
top_issues.stance: As Chief Justice, presides over the Supreme Court and the federal judiciary.

top_issues.name: Administrative Law & Separation of Powers
top_issues.stance: Areas of focus drawn from his appellate practice and tenure.

top_issues.name: Professional Background
top_issues.stance: Former judge on the D.C. Circuit, Deputy Solicitor General, and appellate advocate.

experience.role: Chief Justice of the United States
Judge, U.S. Court of Appeals (D.C. Circuit)
Deputy Solicitor General, U.S. Department of Justice

members.name: Clarence Thomas
members.selection_detail: Nominated by President George H.W. Bush to the seat vacated by Thurgood Marshall. Confirmed 52–48 on Oct 15, 1991.

contact.dc_office: Supreme Court of the United States, 1 First Street NE, Washington, DC 20543

top_issues.name: Constitutional Law & Individual Rights
top_issues.stance: Areas of focus across his tenure as the longest-serving current justice.

top_issues.name: Interpretive Approach
top_issues.stance: Widely associated with an originalist and textualist approach to constitutional interpretation.

top_issues.name: Professional Background
top_issues.stance: Former Chair of the EEOC and judge on the D.C. Circuit.

experience.role: Judge, U.S. Court of Appeals (D.C. Circuit)

members.name: Samuel A. Alito Jr.
members.selection_detail: Nominated by President George W. Bush to the seat vacated by Sandra Day O'Connor. Confirmed 58–42 on Jan 31, 2006.

contact.dc_office: Supreme Court of the United States, 1 First Street NE, Washington, DC 20543

top_issues.name: Criminal Law
top_issues.stance: Area of focus drawn from his career as a federal prosecutor.

top_issues.name: Constitutional Law & Religious Liberty
top_issues.stance: Areas of focus across his tenure.

top_issues.name: Professional Background
top_issues.stance: Former U.S. Attorney for the District of New Jersey and judge on the Third Circuit.

experience.role: Judge, U.S. Court of Appeals (Third Circuit)
Deputy Solicitor General, U.S. Department of Justice
Attorney, U.S. Department of Justice Civil Division

members.name: Sonia Sotomayor
members.selection_detail: Nominated by President Obama to the seat vacated by David Souter. First Hispanic Justice. Confirmed 68–31 on Aug 6, 2009.

contact.dc_office: Supreme Court of the United States, 1 First Street NE, Washington, DC 20543

top_issues.name: Criminal Justice
top_issues.stance: Area of focus drawn from her experience as a prosecutor and trial judge.

top_issues.name: Civil Rights
top_issues.stance: Area of focus across her tenure; the first Latina justice.

top_issues.name: Professional Background
top_issues.stance: Former federal trial judge (S.D.N.Y.) and Second Circuit judge.

experience.role: Judge, U.S. Court of Appeals (Second Circuit)
Judge, New York State Supreme Court
Assistant District Attorney, New York County

members.name: Elena Kagan
members.selection_detail: Nominated by President Obama to the seat vacated by John Paul Stevens. Former U.S. Solicitor General. Confirmed 63–37 on Aug 5, 2010.

contact.dc_office: Supreme Court of the United States, 1 First Street NE, Washington, DC 20543

top_issues.name: Administrative Law
top_issues.stance: Area of focus drawn from her government and academic background.

top_issues.name: Professional Background
top_issues.stance: Former U.S. Solicitor General and Dean of Harvard Law School.

top_issues.name: Statutory Interpretation
top_issues.stance: Known for opinions addressing statutory and regulatory questions.

experience.role: Solicitor General of the United States
Professor of Law, Harvard Law School

members.name: Neil M. Gorsuch
members.selection_detail: Nominated by President Trump to the seat held open after Antonin Scalia's death in 2016. Confirmed 54–45 on Apr 7, 2017.

contact.dc_office: Supreme Court of the United States, 1 First Street NE, Washington, DC 20543

top_issues.name: Statutory Interpretation
top_issues.stance: Known for a textualist approach to reading statutes.

top_issues.name: Federal Indian Law
top_issues.stance: Area of focus including the rights of Native American tribes.

top_issues.name: Professional Background
top_issues.stance: Former judge on the Tenth Circuit and Justice Department official.

experience.role: Judge, U.S. Court of Appeals (Tenth Circuit)
Private Practice Attorney, Boies Schiller Flexner

members.name: Brett M. Kavanaugh
members.selection_detail: Nominated by President Trump to the seat vacated by Anthony Kennedy. Confirmed 50–48 on Oct 6, 2018.

contact.dc_office: Supreme Court of the United States, 1 First Street NE, Washington, DC 20543

top_issues.name: Separation of Powers
top_issues.stance: Area of focus drawn from his appellate and executive-branch experience.

top_issues.name: Administrative Law
top_issues.stance: Area of focus across his tenure.

top_issues.name: Professional Background
top_issues.stance: Former judge on the D.C. Circuit and executive-branch attorney.

experience.role: Judge, U.S. Court of Appeals (D.C. Circuit)

members.name: Amy Coney Barrett
members.selection_detail: Nominated by President Trump to the seat vacated by Ruth Bader Ginsburg's death. Confirmed 52–48 on Oct 26, 2020.

contact.dc_office: Supreme Court of the United States, 1 First Street NE, Washington, DC 20543

top_issues.name: Statutory & Constitutional Interpretation
top_issues.stance: Known for a textualist and originalist interpretive approach.

top_issues.name: Professional Background
top_issues.stance: Former law professor at the University of Notre Dame and judge on the Seventh Circuit.

top_issues.name: Civil Procedure
top_issues.stance: Area of focus drawn from her scholarship and teaching.

experience.role: Judge, U.S. Court of Appeals (Seventh Circuit)
Professor of Law, University of Notre Dame

members.name: Ketanji Brown Jackson
members.selection_detail: Nominated by President Biden to the seat vacated by Stephen Breyer's retirement. First Black woman Justice. Confirmed 53–47 on Apr 7, 2022.

contact.dc_office: Supreme Court of the United States, 1 First Street NE, Washington, DC 20543

top_issues.name: Criminal Law & Sentencing
top_issues.stance: Area of focus drawn from her work as a federal public defender and Sentencing Commission member.

top_issues.name: Professional Background
top_issues.stance: Former federal trial judge (D.D.C.) and D.C. Circuit judge; the first Black woman to serve on the Court.

top_issues.name: Administrative Law
top_issues.stance: Area of focus across her tenure.

experience.role: Judge, U.S. Court of Appeals (D.C. Circuit)
Public Defender, Federal Public Defender's Office (D.C.)

congress._note: Leadership selected at the start of the 119th Congress on Jan 3, 2025. Party breakdown reflects seats at the opening of the Congress and may shift as vacancies, resignations, or party-switches occur.

party_breakdown._note: The two Independents (Sanders-VT, King-ME) caucus with Democrats, giving Republicans a 53–47 working majority.

leadership.name: JD Vance
leadership.selection_detail: Ex officio role of the Vice President under Article I, Section 3 of the U.S. Constitution.

contact.dc_office: U.S. Capitol, Washington, DC 20510

top_issues.name: Presiding Officer of the Senate
top_issues.stance: As Vice President, serves as President of the Senate and casts tie-breaking votes.

top_issues.name: Administration Agenda
top_issues.stance: Advances the administration's legislative priorities in the Senate.

experience.role: Vice President of the United States

leadership.name: Chuck Grassley
leadership.selection_detail: Senator since 1981; elected President pro tempore at the start of the 119th Congress. Traditionally held by the longest-serving member of the majority party.

contact.dc_office: U.S. Capitol, Washington, DC 20510

top_issues.name: Senate Leadership
top_issues.stance: Serves as President pro tempore of the Senate, the most senior member of the majority party and third in the presidential line of succession.

top_issues.name: Judiciary & Oversight
top_issues.stance: A senior member focused on the judiciary and government oversight.

leadership.name: John Thune
leadership.selection_detail: Senator since 2005. Elected Majority Leader by the Senate Republican Conference on Nov 13, 2024 to succeed Mitch McConnell.

contact.dc_office: U.S. Capitol, Washington, DC 20510

top_issues.name: Senate Leadership
top_issues.stance: Serves as Senate Majority Leader, setting the chamber's legislative agenda and floor schedule.

top_issues.name: Legislative Agenda
top_issues.stance: Coordinates the majority's priorities across Senate committees.

leadership.name: John Barrasso
leadership.selection_detail: Senator since 2007; elected Republican Whip by the caucus for the 119th Congress.

contact.dc_office: U.S. Capitol, Washington, DC 20510

top_issues.name: Senate Leadership
top_issues.stance: Serves as Senate Majority Whip, the second-ranking majority position, responsible for counting and marshaling votes.

top_issues.name: Energy & Natural Resources
top_issues.stance: A senior voice on energy and natural-resources policy.

leadership.name: Chuck Schumer
leadership.selection_detail: Senator since 1999; has led Senate Democrats since 2017.

contact.dc_office: U.S. Capitol, Washington, DC 20510

top_issues.name: Senate Leadership
top_issues.stance: Serves as the Senate Democratic (Minority) Leader.

top_issues.name: Legislative Agenda
top_issues.stance: Coordinates the minority party's legislative priorities.

leadership.name: Dick Durbin
leadership.selection_detail: Senator since 1997; has served as Democratic Whip since 2005.

contact.dc_office: U.S. Capitol, Washington, DC 20510

top_issues.name: Senate Leadership
top_issues.stance: Serves as the Senate Democratic (Minority) Whip, the second-ranking minority position.

top_issues.name: Judiciary
top_issues.stance: A senior member of the Senate Judiciary Committee.

party_breakdown._note: Seat counts at the start of the 119th Congress on Jan 3, 2025; shifts with vacancies, special elections, and resignations.

leadership.name: Mike Johnson
leadership.selection_detail: Representative since 2017. Re-elected Speaker on Jan 3, 2025 on the first ballot with 218 votes. Speaker since Oct 2023.

contact.dc_office: U.S. Capitol, Washington, DC 20515

top_issues.name: House Leadership
top_issues.stance: Serves as Speaker of the House, presiding over the chamber, setting its agenda, and standing second in the presidential line of succession.

top_issues.name: Legislative Agenda
top_issues.stance: Coordinates the majority's legislative program.

leadership.name: Steve Scalise
leadership.selection_detail: Representative since 2008. Majority Leader since January 2023.

contact.dc_office: U.S. Capitol, Washington, DC 20515

top_issues.name: House Leadership
top_issues.stance: Serves as House Majority Leader, managing the floor schedule and the majority's legislative program.

top_issues.stance: Coordinates the majority's priorities across House committees.

leadership.name: Tom Emmer
leadership.selection_detail: Representative since 2015. Majority Whip since January 2023.

contact.dc_office: U.S. Capitol, Washington, DC 20515

top_issues.name: House Leadership
top_issues.stance: Serves as House Majority Whip, the third-ranking majority position, responsible for counting and organizing votes.

experience.role: Majority Whip, U.S. House of Representatives

leadership.name: Lisa McClain
leadership.selection_detail: Representative since 2021; elected Conference Chair for the 119th Congress.

contact.dc_office: U.S. Capitol, Washington, DC 20515

top_issues.name: House Leadership
top_issues.stance: Serves as Chair of the House Republican Conference, the party's member-coordination and messaging role.

leadership.name: Hakeem Jeffries
leadership.selection_detail: Representative since 2013. Has led House Democrats since January 2023 — first Black leader of a major party in Congress.

contact.dc_office: U.S. Capitol, Washington, DC 20515

leadership.name: Katherine Clark
leadership.selection_detail: Representative since 2013. Minority Whip since January 2023.

leadership.name: Pete Aguilar
leadership.selection_detail: Representative since 2015; re-elected Caucus Chair for the 119th Congress.

elections._note: Upcoming federal election cycles. State-specific ballot data lives in /data/{state}/elections.json.

upcoming.name: 2026 U.S. Midterm Elections
upcoming.description: All 435 seats in the U.S. House of Representatives and 33 of 100 U.S. Senate seats are up for regular election. Various special elections may also occur.

upcoming.name: 2028 U.S. Presidential Election
upcoming.description: Quadrennial presidential election. Incumbent Donald J. Trump is term-limited under the 22nd Amendment and may not seek a further term.
